skill.name: codebase-cleanup-tech-debt
skill.description: Guides technical debt analysis by inventorying code, architecture, testing, documentation, and infrastructure issues with impact and remediation plans.
skill.summary: Analyze technical debt and create measurable cleanup roadmaps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but manual review found only Markdown prose and fenced examples in SKILL.md. No executable scripts, prompt injection, secret access, network exfiltration, or malicious intent were found.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Ruby Backtick Execution Detections
low_findings.description: The reported external command detections are false positives in Markdown code fences and example blocks. They show sample debt reports, YAML, Python, and Markdown output, not Ruby backtick execution or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows these hits are Markdown fenced examples or prose. There is no Ruby source, command runner, or instruction to execute user-controlled shell input.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Detections
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits are false positives caused by ordinary words such as description, Design, guides, and Design. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic operation appears at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter and technical debt guidance text. Manual review found no weak algorithm usage or cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Dismissed System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance hit is a false positive on an output-format item about creating a prevention plan. It does not request host inspection, environment discovery, network scanning, or credential collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 383 is a report section label within a Markdown output format list. No reconnaissance command or semantic intent to inspect the system is present.

content.value_statement: Technical debt slows delivery, increases defects, and makes maintenance harder to plan. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code inventory debt, quantify impact, and build practical remediation plans.
actual_capabilities: Identifies code, architecture, testing, documentation, and infrastructure debt categories.
Defines metrics for complexity, duplication, coverage, dependency health, and delivery impact.
Estimates cost, risk, and ROI for technical debt items.
Creates quick-win, medium-term, and long-term remediation plans.
Suggests prevention gates for reviews, CI, testing, and documentation.
Structures final reports with inventory, impact, roadmap, and success metrics.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not an automated static analysis engine.
It depends on available repository context and project history.
Cost estimates require team-specific assumptions to be accurate.
It does not directly run cleanup tools or modify code by itself.

use_cases.title: Plan A Cleanup Sprint
use_cases.description: Identify high-impact cleanup work that can fit into the next sprint.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Refactoring Work
use_cases.description: Rank refactoring candidates by risk, effort, and expected developer velocity gain.

use_cases.title: Prepare A Debt Report
use_cases.description: Create a clear technical debt summary for planning, budgeting, or stakeholder review.

prompt_templates.title: Find Technical Debt
prompt_templates.prompt: Use codebase-cleanup-tech-debt to review this project. Identify the main technical debt categories and summarize the highest-risk items.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Cleanup Backlog
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the codebase for technical debt and create a prioritized cleanup backlog with effort, impact, and recommended owners.

prompt_templates.title: Estimate Debt Cost
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to estimate the monthly cost of our technical debt. Include velocity impact, bug cost, risk level, and ROI for remediation.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Remediation Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a full technical debt assessment. Produce a quarter-by-quarter remediation roadmap, prevention gates, success metrics, and stakeholder summary.

output_examples.input: Review our backend service for technical debt before sprint planning.
output: Grouped debt into code complexity, testing gaps, dependency health, and deployment issues.
Ranked duplicate validation logic and missing integration tests as the highest-impact items.
Proposed three quick wins for the next sprint with estimated effort and savings.

output_examples.input: Help me explain why we need time for refactoring.
output: Prepared a stakeholder summary that links debt to delivery delay and defect cost.
Included a remediation investment estimate and expected ROI timeline.
Recommended measurable success metrics for monthly review.

output_examples.input: Create a prevention plan so new debt does not keep growing.
output: Recommended quality gates for complexity, duplication, coverage, dependency audits, and architecture checks.
Defined review expectations for tests and documentation.
Suggested a quarterly debt budget and progress review process.

best_practices: Provide repository context, recent pain points, and known delivery bottlenecks.
Validate cost estimates with team-specific rates, bug data, and deployment history.
Convert recommendations into small tracked work items with owners and metrics.
anti_patterns: Using the report as a replacement for direct code review or testing.
Ranking debt by annoyance instead of measurable impact and risk.
Starting large rewrites without quick wins, safety tests, or rollout plans.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically scan code?
faq.answer: No. It guides the assistant through a structured assessment using available project context and files.

faq.question: Can it help justify cleanup work to stakeholders?
faq.answer: Yes. It frames debt in terms of cost, risk, ROI, and delivery impact.

faq.question: What types of debt does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers code, architecture, testing, documentation, infrastructure, dependency, and process debt.

faq.question: Is it useful for small projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It can focus on quick wins, missing tests, duplicate logic, and simple prevention gates.

faq.question: Does it make code changes?
faq.answer: No. It creates analysis and plans. Code changes require separate implementation steps.

faq.question: How should teams use the output?
faq.answer: Teams should review findings, confirm assumptions, create backlog items, and track progress with clear metrics.
